---
title: "Hard Disk Problem?"
date: 2007-08-08
forum: Absolute Beginner Talk
---

### Post by m4rk6 on 2007-08-08
This is with Kubuntu Feisty.
I mounted a second hard drive with 3 partitions. Everytime I try to transfer data to or from it, the transfer stalls and then I can't see any files.

fstab:
> 
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config --
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/hda1 :
UUID=f1b61b11-6fff-4e9e-88ed-2061fb190752 / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/hda5 :
UUID=11f1bd6d-4373-4cbd-a219-5c8ca60c48ea none swap sw 0 0
/dev/hdc /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto 0 0
/dev/sda1 /home/markg/xternal/movies ext3 defaults 0 0
/dev/sda2 /home/markg/xternal/music ext3 defaults 0 0
/dev/sda3 /home/markg/xternal/misc ext3 defaults 0 0

whenever that happens I do
> sudo umount -a

and then
> sudo mount -a

and I get this:
> mount: wrong fs type, bad option, bad superblock on /dev/sda1,
       missing codepage or other error
       In some cases useful info is found in syslog - try
       dmesg | tail  or so

mount: wrong fs type, bad option, bad superblock on /dev/sda2,
       missing codepage or other error
       In some cases useful info is found in syslog - try
       dmesg | tail  or so

mount: wrong fs type, bad option, bad superblock on /dev/sda3,
       missing codepage or other error
       In some cases useful info is found in syslog - try
       dmesg | tail  or so


I formatted the partitions, so I know they use Linux ext3 filesystems. The hard disk has no problems whatsoever, what's wrong?

---

### Post by meierfra on 2007-08-08
Please post the output of

```
sudo fdisk -l
```

---

### Post by m4rk6 on 2007-08-08
sudo fdisk -l
> Disk /dev/sda: 80.0 GB, 80026361856 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 9729 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/sda1               1        3824    30716248+  83  Linux
/dev/sda2            3825        7648    30716280   83  Linux
/dev/sda3            7649        9729    16715632+  83  Linux

Disk /dev/hda: 120.0 GB, 120034123776 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 14593 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/hda1   *           1       14216   114189988+  83  Linux
/dev/hda2           14217       14593     3028252+   5  Extended
/dev/hda5           14217       14593     3028221   82  Linux swap / Solaris


---

### Post by m4rk6 on 2007-08-09
... Is this an example of how stable linux is?

---

### Post by tgalati4 on 2007-08-09
Post the output of:

>dmesg | tail -n 30

Also, replace the UUID=blahblah statements with the actual device names listed above each line in /etc/fstab.

You will also have to modify your /boot/grub/menu.lst to reflect the actual drive in the root=UUID=blahblah statement.

---

### Post by m4rk6 on 2007-08-09
when you say replace UUID=blahblah does that mean this:
> # Entry for /dev/hda1 :
UUID=f1b61b11-6fff-4e9e-88ed-2061fb190752 / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/hda5 :
UUID=11f1bd6d-4373-4cbd-a219-5c8ca60c48ea none swap sw 0 0
---to---
# Entry for /dev/hda1 :
UUID=**/dev/hda1** / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/hda5 :
UUID=**/dev/hda5** none swap sw 0 0

?

---

### Post by meierfra on 2007-08-09
Yes, I'm pretty sure that's what he wants you do.

the dmesg command he posted might give some useful error messages, so it would be good to see them.

---
